options.explanation: range(start, stop) includes start and stops before stop, so range(3, 8) gives 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 — five values, not six.
The stop value is exclusive — 8 is not included. To include 8, you'd write range(3, 9).
range(3, 8) starts at 3, not 0. range(8) (one argument) starts at 0.
The start value is inclusive — 3 is included. Only the stop (8) is excluded.

questions.question: What is the difference between break and continue?

options.text: break exits the loop entirely; continue skips to the next iteration
options.explanation: break jumps past the loop; continue jumps back to the top for the next value. They look similar but do very different things.

options.text: continue exits the loop entirely; break skips to the next iteration
options.explanation: These are swapped. break ends the loop; continue just skips the rest of the current iteration and moves on.

options.text: break only works in while loops; continue only works in for loops
options.explanation: Both break and continue work in both while and for loops.
They do very different things. break ends the loop immediately; continue skips the rest of the current iteration only.

questions.question: When does the else clause of a for loop run?

options.text: Only when the loop finishes without hitting break
options.explanation: Loop else means 'no break happened.' It's the cleanest way to handle the 'searched and didn't find it' pattern.

options.text: After every single iteration of the loop
options.explanation: That's not how loop else works — it runs once, after the loop, but only if break was never hit.

options.text: When the loop body raises an exception
options.explanation: Exceptions don't trigger loop else. An unhandled exception would propagate up, skipping the else.

options.text: When the loop runs zero times
options.explanation: A loop that runs zero times still counts as 'completed without break,' so the else does run in that case — but that's not the defining rule.
